metadata.description: Groq-optimized knowledge base for workout assistant chatbot. Structured for fast retrieval and concise context injection.
metadata.usage_note: Derived from master-knowledge-base.txt. Do not edit directly — regenerate from master.

system_prompt_context: You are {{username}}'s personal workout assistant. You have deep knowledge of evidence-based training and nutrition. You also have access to the user's actual workout data from their app (logged workouts, splits, exercise history, progress over time, profile info). Use this data to give personalized advice. Be direct, specific, and encouraging. When the user's data shows patterns (e.g., skipping muscle groups, declining volume, good progression), mention them proactively. Never reference images or photos. Identify yourself as '{{username}}'s personal workout assistant'.

topics.title: Training Priority Hierarchy
topics.summary: Training variables ranked by importance: 1) Safety & Enjoyment → Sustainability, 2) Effort (RPE 7-9, within 2-3 reps of failure), 3) Progressive Overload (the #1 driver of growth — add weight or reps over time) & Prioritization (train weak points first), 4) Volume (10-20 hard sets/muscle/week), 5) Frequency (2x/week per body part minimum), 6) Rep ranges (wide range works: 6-15 main, 3-6 for strength, 15-25 for accessories), 7) Exercise selection (compounds first), 8) Rest periods (2-5 min compounds, 1-2 min isolation), 9) Tempo (least important — just control the weight).
key_facts: Progressive overload is the #1 driver of long-term muscle growth
Muscle confusion is a myth — muscles respond to progressive tension, not exercise novelty
Train within 2-3 reps of failure on most sets (RPE 7-9)
Absolute failure every set is NOT required and may impair recovery
Sustainability beats optimization — the best program is one you'll follow
10-20 hard sets per muscle group per week is the research-supported range
Training each muscle 2x/week beats 1x/week even at the same total volume
Hypertrophy occurs across a wide rep range (3-30+) if effort is matched
Tempo is the least important variable — don't overthink it

topics.title: Training Splits
topics.summary: Three main splits recommended based on experience and schedule.

splits.name: Upper/Lower
splits.schedule: Upper, Lower, Rest, Upper, Lower, Rest, Rest

splits.name: Push/Pull/Legs
splits.best_for: Intermediates who want more gym time
splits.schedule: Push, Pull, Legs, Push, Pull, Legs, Rest

splits.name: Full Body
splits.schedule: Full Body, Rest, Full Body, Rest, Full Body, Rest, Rest

topics.title: Recommended Exercises by Body Part

hamstrings_glutes: Leg curl (essential — squats barely hit hamstrings)

principles: Compound movements first, isolation work for lagging areas
Cover all movement patterns: push, pull, squat, hinge
Keep main lifts consistent for tracking; rotate accessories
Choose exercises you enjoy and can perform safely

topics.title: Rest Periods

recommendations.supersets: Great time-saver — pair opposing muscles (e.g., bicep curl + tricep pushdown)

topics.key_point: Longer rest allows better volume maintenance, which drives more hypertrophy. Don't rush heavy compounds.

topics.title: Nutrition Fundamentals

macros.protein: 0.7-1.0g per lb bodyweight (most important macro). Spread across 3-5 meals.
macros.carbs: 40-55% of calories. Primary fuel for training. Don't fear carbs.
macros.fats: 20-35% of calories. Essential for hormones. Don't go below 0.3g/lb.

practical_tips: Track food for a few weeks to learn portions — don't need to track forever
No foods are inherently 'bad' — focus on overall daily intake
Flexible dieting works: hit protein and calorie targets, fill rest with foods you enjoy
Hydration: ~half bodyweight (lbs) in oz of water daily

topics.title: Supplements
worth_taking: Creatine monohydrate: 3-5g daily (most well-researched, safe long-term)
Protein powder: convenient for hitting protein targets (not magic)
not_worth_taking: BCAAs (waste of money if protein intake is adequate)
Fat burners (minimal effect, not worth side effects)

topics.title: Recovery & Lifestyle
topics.sleep: 7-9 hours/night. Most muscle repair occurs during sleep. Consistent schedule > total hours.
topics.deloads: Every 4-8 weeks, reduce volume/intensity by 40-50%. Beginners may not need deloads for first several months.
topics.warmup: 5-10 min light cardio, dynamic stretching, 1-2 lighter warm-up sets before working sets.
topics.stress: Chronic stress elevates cortisol and impairs recovery. Manage with meditation, walks, hobbies.

topics.title: Volume Concepts (Advanced)

concepts.MEV: Minimum Effective Volume — least amount needed to grow (~6-8 sets/muscle/week)
concepts.MAV: Maximum Adaptive Volume — the sweet spot (~10-15 sets/muscle/week)
concepts.MRV: Maximum Recoverable Volume — most you can do and recover from (~20-25 sets/muscle/week)

topics.key_point: Volume has diminishing returns. Doubling sets does NOT double gains. Going beyond MRV leads to overtraining.

topics.title: Rep Ranges & Periodization
topics.hypertrophy_range: Broadly 3-30+ reps can build muscle IF close to failure

practical_ranges.hypertrophy_main: 6-12 reps (good blend of size and strength)

topics.periodization: Can vary rep ranges across blocks. Accumulation phase (higher reps, more volume) → Intensification phase (lower reps, heavier weight). Not critical for beginners.

topics.title: Lifting Tempo
topics.recommendation: Moderate tempo: 2-6 seconds per rep total. Eccentric 2-3 sec (controlled), concentric 1-2 sec (can be explosive).
topics.avoid: Very slow reps (10+ sec per rep) — reduce volume without extra benefit.
topics.technique: Enhanced eccentrics (3-5 sec lowering) can be useful for hypertrophy but are fatiguing. Use strategically.
topics.priority: Tempo is the LEAST important training variable. Focus on the big rocks first.
